events.label: reasoning
events.text:
The scenario describes sawcut joints being done late, resulting in random cracking. This is a concrete finishing & curing issue. Sawcut contraction joints must be cut at the right time—typically within 12-24 hours after finishing—before random shrinkage cracking occurs. Cutting too late means the concrete has already cracked randomly, defeating the purpose of the joints.

This is a defective condition. The joints were installed but too late, and random cracking has occurred. This is non-compliant with ACI 302/308 guidance on jointing.

Decision: fail (non-compliant/defective)
Risk: medium (random cracking affects durability and appearance, may need repair)
Work stage: rough-complete
Component conditions: installed-defective, non-compliant
Percent complete: maybe 85
Value: 0
Confidence: 0.9
Findings: random cracking due to delayed sawcutting of contraction joints
Actions: evaluate cracks, repair/epoxy injection as needed, review joint timing for remaining pours
References: ACI 302, ACI 308
